stem:
A 29-year-old G1P0 attends her 26-week antenatal appointment after her routine 75 g oral glucose tolerance test. She has been well, is gaining weight along expected centiles, and has no symptoms. Her booking BMI was 26 kg/m^2. She has no family history of diabetes and is not on any regular medications.

Her fasting plasma glucose was 4.8 mmol/L. The 1-hour value was 10.4 mmol/L. The 2-hour value was 7.8 mmol/L. Her HbA1c at booking was 5.0%. Her urinalysis today is negative for glucose and protein.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 80, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 100%, temperature 36.6 °C, foetal HR 145 bpm
data_table.Examination: Fundal height appropriate for dates, normal symphysis-fundal growth chart, no oedema
data_table.Investigations: OGTT fasting 4.8 mmol/L, 1-hour 10.4 mmol/L, 2-hour 7.8 mmol/L, booking HbA1c 5.0%, urinalysis negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate interpretation of these results?

options.text: Normal glucose tolerance because the fasting and 2-hour values are below threshold
options.rationale: ADIPS criteria treat any single threshold breach as diagnostic; a normal fasting and 2-hour result does not override an elevated 1-hour result. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines for GDM 2014)

options.text: Gestational diabetes diagnosed on the 1-hour OGTT value alone, no repeat needed
options.rationale: ADIPS Australian criteria diagnose GDM when ANY single OGTT value meets the threshold: fasting ≥5.1, 1-hour ≥10.0, or 2-hour ≥8.5 mmol/L; this patient's 1-hour 10.4 mmol/L is diagnostic. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines for GDM 2014)

options.text: Repeat the OGTT in 4 weeks because only one value is elevated on this test
options.rationale: ADIPS does not require repeat OGTT after a single elevated value; repeating delays management and increases foetal exposure to hyperglycaemia. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines for GDM 2014)

options.text: Pre-existing type 2 diabetes because the HbA1c at booking was above 5.5%
options.rationale: Her booking HbA1c of 5.0% is well below the type 2 diabetes diagnostic threshold of 6.5% and is also below the early-pregnancy GDM threshold of 5.9%. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines for GDM 2014)

options.text: Impaired glucose tolerance only, not gestational diabetes, on these OGTT values
options.rationale: Australian pregnancy guidance does not use an 'impaired glucose tolerance' category separate from GDM during pregnancy; any single OGTT breach is GDM. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines for GDM 2014)

explanation.summary: Australian GDM diagnosis is based on the 75 g OGTT performed at 24-28 weeks, with ADIPS thresholds of fasting ≥5.1, 1-hour ≥10.0, and 2-hour ≥8.5 mmol/L. Any single elevated value is diagnostic. Earlier OGTT (booking) is recommended for women with risk factors (prior GDM, BMI ≥30, prior macrosomic baby, ethnicity from a high-prevalence group, age ≥40, family history). Once diagnosed, first-line management is dietary advice and self-monitoring; insulin or metformin is added when glycaemic targets are not met.
key_points: ADIPS OGTT thresholds: fasting ≥5.1, 1-h ≥10.0, 2-h ≥8.5 mmol/L.
Any single elevated value is diagnostic; no repeat required.
Early OGTT for high-risk women at booking.
First-line management is diet and SMBG; insulin or metformin if targets unmet.
explanation.pearls: ADIPS: GDM diagnosed by a single OGTT value at or above fasting 5.1, 1-h 10.0, or 2-h 8.5 mmol/L.

stem:
A 31-year-old G2P0 presents to the early pregnancy unit with 2 weeks of intermittent right iliac fossa discomfort. Her last menstrual period was 7 weeks ago. She has no syncope, no shoulder-tip pain, and no significant bleeding. She lives 20 minutes from the hospital and can attend twice-weekly follow-up.

Transvaginal ultrasound shows a 3.1 cm right tubal mass without foetal cardiac activity and no free fluid. Beta-hCG is 4200 IU/L. Her LFTs and UEC are within normal limits. She has had no prior abdominal surgery and no contraindications to methotrexate.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 76, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 100%, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, mild right adnexal tenderness, no peritonism, no cervical motion tenderness
data_table.Investigations: TV ultrasound 3.1 cm right tubal mass, no foetal cardiac activity, no free fluid, beta-hCG 4200 IU/L, UEC and LFTs within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management option?

options.text: Emergency laparotomy with salpingectomy regardless of haemodynamic stability
options.rationale: Laparotomy is reserved for haemodynamic instability or where laparoscopy is not feasible; this stable patient with mild symptoms does not need open surgery. (Source: RANZCOG Tubal Ectopic Pregnancy Statement 2024)

options.text: Laparoscopic salpingectomy of the right tube as definitive surgical management
options.rationale: Salpingectomy is preferred when surgery is needed but this patient meets RANZCOG criteria for medical management; surgery here over-treats and removes a tube whose future fertility role matters. (Source: RANZCOG Tubal Ectopic Pregnancy Statement 2024)

options.text: Laparoscopic salpingostomy with conservation of the right tube and bhCG follow-up
options.rationale: Salpingostomy is the surgical option chosen when the contralateral tube is absent or damaged; with a normal opposite tube and methotrexate criteria met, surgery is not indicated. (Source: RANZCOG Tubal Ectopic Pregnancy Statement 2024)

options.text: Expectant management with serial beta-hCG and no methotrexate or surgery
options.rationale: Expectant management is acceptable only with bhCG <1000-1500 IU/L and falling, low symptom burden, and small mass; at bhCG 4200 IU/L the risk of progression and tubal rupture is too high. (Source: RANZCOG Tubal Ectopic Pregnancy Statement 2024)

options.text: Single-dose intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg/m^2 with serial beta-hCG monitoring
options.rationale: RANZCOG GPS criteria for methotrexate include beta-hCG <5000 IU/L, mass <3.5 cm, no foetal cardiac activity, no significant pain, reliable follow-up, and no contraindications; this patient meets all six. (Source: RANZCOG Tubal Ectopic Pregnancy Statement 2024)

explanation.summary: Tubal ectopic pregnancy management in Australia follows RANZCOG GPS criteria. Methotrexate is preferred when beta-hCG <5000 IU/L, mass <3.5 cm, no foetal cardiac activity, no significant pain, reliable follow-up access, and no contraindications (active hepatic or renal disease, immunodeficiency, peptic ulcer, breastfeeding, or known sensitivity). Surgery is indicated for higher bhCG, large mass, foetal cardiac activity, significant pain, haemodynamic instability, or failed medical therapy. Salpingectomy is preferred over salpingostomy when the contralateral tube is healthy.
key_points: Methotrexate threshold (RANZCOG): bhCG <5000 IU/L plus the full criteria set.
Surgical management when bhCG ≥5000, mass ≥3.5 cm, FCA present, significant pain, or instability.
Salpingectomy preferred over salpingostomy when the opposite tube is healthy.
Day 4 and day 7 bhCG: ≥15% fall expected with successful methotrexate.
explanation.pearls: RANZCOG threshold for ectopic surgery vs methotrexate is bhCG 5000 IU/L (not 3000), with the full criteria set met.

stem:
A 32-year-old G2P2 presents on day 4 after a forceps-assisted vaginal delivery with a 12-hour history of fever and lower abdominal pain. She had prolonged rupture of membranes (28 hours) before delivery. Her lochia is heavy and foul-smelling.

On arrival to the postnatal ward she is alert and orientated. There is no calf swelling, no respiratory symptoms, and no flank pain. The uterus is tender on bimanual examination at the level of the umbilicus. Initial bloods show a raised CRP and a left shift on FBC.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 118, BP 102/64 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 98%, temperature 38.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Tender boggy uterus at umbilicus, foul-smelling lochia, no calf swelling, chest clear, mild suprapubic tenderness without peritonism
data_table.Investigations: WCC 18.4 × 10^9/L (4-11), CRP 145 mg/L (<5), lactate 1.8 mmol/L, urine MCS sent, blood cultures sent

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Computed tomography abdomen and pelvis prior to starting any antibiotic therapy
options.rationale: Imaging is reserved for suspected abscess, retained products, or atypical course; empiric antibiotics should not be delayed for CT in a febrile tachycardic postpartum woman. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Oral cephalexin 500 mg four times daily as outpatient treatment for 7 days
options.rationale: Oral monotherapy with a first-generation cephalosporin does not cover anaerobes or enterococci and is inadequate for febrile inpatient endometritis with tachycardia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g eight-hourly as broad-spectrum monotherapy
options.rationale: Piperacillin-tazobactam is broader-spectrum than needed for first-line endometritis; the AU step-up regimen is amox-gent-metronidazole, with piperacillin-tazobactam reserved for non-response or sepsis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin 25 mg/kg load then 15 mg/kg twelve-hourly as monotherapy
options.rationale: Vancomycin covers MRSA and gram-positive organisms but misses gram-negatives and anaerobes; it is not first-line empirical therapy for endometritis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Intravenous amoxicillin, gentamicin, and metronidazole until afebrile for 24 hours
options.rationale: Therapeutic Guidelines empirical regimen for puerperal endometritis is IV ampicillin or amoxicillin plus gentamicin plus metronidazole to cover polymicrobial genital tract organisms including anaerobes. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Puerperal endometritis is a polymicrobial ascending genital tract infection that presents with fever, uterine tenderness, and offensive lochia in the first 10 days postpartum. Risk factors include caesarean section, prolonged rupture of membranes, instrumental delivery, and chorioamnionitis. The AU empirical regimen is IV amoxicillin or ampicillin plus gentamicin plus metronidazole, switched to oral once afebrile for 24-48 hours. Imaging is reserved for failed response, retained products, or suspected abscess. Septic pelvic thrombophlebitis is a differential when fever persists despite antibiotics.
key_points: AU empirical: IV amoxicillin or ampicillin plus gentamicin plus metronidazole.
Cover polymicrobial including anaerobes (genital tract flora).
Switch to oral once afebrile 24-48 hours; total 7-14 days.
Failed response should prompt imaging for retained products or abscess.
explanation.pearls: Postpartum endometritis: IV amoxicillin plus gentamicin plus metronidazole; persisting fever after 48 h suggests retained products or septic pelvic thrombophlebitis.

stem:
A 34-year-old G2P1 is seen in the antenatal clinic at 32 weeks gestation to discuss mode of delivery. Her first pregnancy was 3 years ago at 35 weeks for severe early pre-eclampsia. The operation note from that admission records 'extension of lower segment incision required, vertical extension up the uterine body, repaired in two layers'.

This pregnancy has been uncomplicated. The foetus is cephalic, AFI 14 cm, EFW 50th centile. Placenta is fundal. She has been counselled about VBAC versus elective caesarean and asks for your advice.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 100%, temperature 36.6 °C, foetal HR 142 bpm
data_table.Examination: Fundal height 32 cm, cephalic presentation, no scar tenderness
data_table.Investigations: Ultrasound at 32 weeks: fundal anterior placenta, AFI 14 cm, EFW 50th centile, foetal Dopplers normal

lead_in: Which feature is the strongest absolute contraindication to attempting VBAC?

options.text: Estimated foetal weight on the 50th centile at 32 weeks gestation review today
options.rationale: A normally grown foetus is not a contraindication to VBAC; macrosomia (>4 kg estimated) is a relative concern but not absolute. (Source: RANZCOG Birth After Previous Caesarean Section Statement 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Birth After Previous Caesarean Section Statement 2023

options.text: Maternal age 34 with a body mass index of 28 kg/m^2 at booking visit
options.rationale: Maternal age and overweight BMI modestly reduce VBAC success rates but neither is an absolute contraindication to attempted vaginal birth after caesarean. (Source: RANZCOG Birth After Previous Caesarean Section Statement 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Birth After Previous Caesarean Section Statement 2023

options.text: A single previous lower-segment caesarean section in the immediately prior pregnancy
options.rationale: A single previous LSCS is the standard indication for VBAC counselling and is not a contraindication; success rates are 60-80% with no upper-segment scar. (Source: RANZCOG Birth After Previous Caesarean Section Statement 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Birth After Previous Caesarean Section Statement 2023

options.text: Previous classical (vertical) uterine incision documented in the operation note
options.rationale: RANZCOG lists previous classical or T-shaped uterine incision as an absolute contraindication because the upper-segment myometrium has a uterine-rupture risk of 4-9% in labour. (Source: RANZCOG Birth After Previous Caesarean Section Statement 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Birth After Previous Caesarean Section Statement 2023

options.text: Past medical history of severe early-onset pre-eclampsia in the first pregnancy
options.rationale: Previous pre-eclampsia is an indication for aspirin and surveillance in this pregnancy but is not by itself a contraindication to attempting VBAC in a low-risk uncomplicated current pregnancy. (Source: RANZCOG Birth After Previous Caesarean Section Statement 2023)
source_refs: RANZCOG Birth After Previous Caesarean Section Statement 2023

explanation.summary: Vaginal birth after caesarean is recommended in suitable candidates because of lower morbidity than repeat caesarean, but it requires a low-segment scar in the prior surgery. Absolute contraindications include previous classical or T-shaped (upper-segment) incision, previous uterine rupture, more than two prior caesareans, placenta praevia or vasa praevia, and any other contraindication to vaginal birth. Relative factors that lower success include higher BMI, advanced maternal age, induction with prostaglandins, and short interpregnancy interval. Counselling must include uterine rupture risk of approximately 0.5% with one prior LSCS.
key_points: Absolute contraindications: classical/T incision, prior rupture, >2 caesareans, praevia/vasa praevia.
Single previous LSCS: VBAC offered with informed counselling; success 60-80%.
Uterine rupture risk with one prior LSCS ~0.5%.
Prostaglandin induction increases rupture risk; mechanical methods preferred.
explanation.pearls: RANZCOG: previous classical or T-shaped uterine incision is an absolute VBAC contraindication; rupture risk 4-9% versus ~0.5% with low-segment scar.

sources.label: RANZCOG Birth After Previous Caesarean Section Statement 2023
NHMRC Mode of birth after caesarean

stem:
A 32-year-old G3P2 at 40+2 weeks is brought into the birth suite by ambulance with spontaneous rupture of membranes 30 minutes ago. Her previous two pregnancies were uncomplicated vaginal deliveries. She declined external cephalic version at 37 weeks because she was not comfortable with the procedure. Her last ultrasound at 38 weeks showed a footling breech.

On arrival vaginal examination reveals 4 cm dilated cervix with a foot palpable below the cord and no cord prolapse identified. CTG is reassuring at baseline 140 bpm. The on-call obstetrician and paediatric team are present.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 92, BP 122/76 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.6 °C, foetal HR 140 bpm
data_table.Examination: Cervix 4 cm dilated, footling breech with right foot palpable through cervix, no cord prolapse, contractions every 4 minutes
data_table.Investigations: Bedside ultrasound confirms footling breech, EFW 3.6 kg, AFI 6 cm post-SROM, no nuchal cord visualised

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Induction of labour with augmentation by oxytocin and continuous foetal monitoring
options.rationale: Augmenting a footling breech increases cord prolapse and uterine hyperstimulation risk and does not solve the underlying contraindication to vaginal breech birth. (Source: RANZCOG Breech Presentation at Term Statement 2022)
source_refs: RANZCOG Breech Presentation at Term Statement 2022

options.text: Vaginal breech delivery with epidural analgesia and experienced obstetric assistance
options.rationale: Vaginal breech delivery has acceptable outcomes for selected frank or complete breech with strict criteria; footling breech is specifically excluded because of cord prolapse and head entrapment risk. (Source: RANZCOG Breech Presentation at Term Statement 2022)
source_refs: RANZCOG Breech Presentation at Term Statement 2022

options.text: Emergency caesarean section as soon as the operating theatre is available
options.rationale: RANZCOG and the Term Breech Trial findings make planned caesarean the safer route for footling breech in particular because of much higher cord prolapse and head entrapment risk than frank or complete breech. (Source: RANZCOG Breech Presentation at Term Statement 2022)
source_refs: RANZCOG Breech Presentation at Term Statement 2022

options.text: External cephalic version now under bedside ultrasound guidance after SROM
options.rationale: ECV is contraindicated after spontaneous rupture of membranes due to reduced amniotic fluid and increased risk of cord prolapse and abruption during the manipulation. (Source: RANZCOG Breech Presentation at Term Statement 2022)
source_refs: RANZCOG Breech Presentation at Term Statement 2022

options.text: Internal podalic version with breech extraction by the on-call obstetric registrar
options.rationale: Internal podalic version is reserved for the second twin with non-cephalic presentation in a planned vaginal birth; it has no role in a singleton footling breech in active labour. (Source: RANZCOG Breech Presentation at Term Statement 2022)
source_refs: RANZCOG Breech Presentation at Term Statement 2022

explanation.summary: Footling breech presentation in singleton pregnancies at term carries higher risk than frank or complete breech because of cord prolapse with each contraction and trapped after-coming head when only one or two limbs lead. Even centres that still offer planned vaginal breech birth for selected frank or complete breeches exclude footling breech. After SROM with the cord at risk, the operative pathway is rapid caesarean section. ECV is contraindicated once membranes are ruptured. Internal podalic version belongs to second-twin delivery, not singleton breech at term.
key_points: Footling breech is an absolute exclusion from planned vaginal breech birth.
Cord prolapse risk is high enough that delivery should be expedited.
ECV is contraindicated after SROM.
Internal podalic version is a second-twin manoeuvre, not singleton.
explanation.pearls: Footling breech with SROM = emergency caesarean; cord prolapse and head entrapment risk excludes vaginal breech in this subtype.

sources.label: RANZCOG Breech Presentation at Term Statement 2022

stem:
A 28-year-old presents for a planning visit at 8 weeks gestation in her first pregnancy. She has had recurrent major depressive disorder for 6 years and has been well on sertraline 100 mg daily for 18 months. Two previous depressive episodes responded well to sertraline. She has had no manic or psychotic features.

She is unsure whether to continue medication. She has a supportive partner and a stable job. There is no recent suicidal ideation. Her partner is also concerned about possible foetal risks and asks for evidence-based advice.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78, BP 116/72 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.6 °C, foetal HR 160 bpm
data_table.Examination: Mood euthymic, no psychomotor changes, normal reasoning, no suicidal ideation
data_table.Investigations: Booking bloods within normal limits; viability ultrasound shows single intrauterine pregnancy at 8 weeks

lead_in: What is the most appropriate medication recommendation?

options.text: Switch to mirtazapine 15 mg nocte to avoid SSRI exposure to the foetus completely
options.rationale: Mirtazapine has less pregnancy safety data than sertraline and is not preferred when a patient is already responding well to a first-line SSRI. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Clinical Practice Guideline 2023)
source_refs: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Clinical Practice Guideline 2023

options.text: Switch to paroxetine 20 mg daily for a smoother antidepressant profile in pregnancy
options.rationale: Paroxetine is associated with a small increased risk of cardiac malformations in first-trimester exposure and is not preferred when other SSRIs are options. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Clinical Practice Guideline 2023)
source_refs: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Clinical Practice Guideline 2023

options.text: Cross-taper to fluoxetine 20 mg daily during the first trimester for safety reasons
options.rationale: Fluoxetine has a long half-life and active metabolite that increases neonatal adaptation syndrome risk; sertraline is preferred when already effective. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Clinical Practice Guideline 2023)
source_refs: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Clinical Practice Guideline 2023

options.text: Stop sertraline now and offer psychological therapy alone for the entire pregnancy
options.rationale: Stopping a well-controlled SSRI in recurrent depression carries a 60-70% relapse risk within 6 months, with associated obstetric morbidity and infant outcomes. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Clinical Practice Guideline 2023)
source_refs: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Clinical Practice Guideline 2023

options.text: Continue sertraline 100 mg daily at the current effective antidepressant dose
options.rationale: Sertraline has the most extensive AU pregnancy evidence base and is the preferred SSRI antenatally; continuing an effective regimen avoids relapse, which is itself a foetal and maternal risk. (Source: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Clinical Practice Guideline 2023)
source_refs: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Clinical Practice Guideline 2023

explanation.summary: Antenatal antidepressant decisions weigh untreated maternal depression risks (preterm birth, low birth weight, postnatal depression, impaired attachment) against medication risks. The Australian RANZCP perinatal guideline supports continuing an effective SSRI through pregnancy where the woman has a history of recurrent depression. Sertraline is the preferred first-line SSRI in pregnancy; paroxetine is avoided where possible. Fluoxetine is acceptable when already effective but is more associated with neonatal adaptation syndrome. Multidisciplinary perinatal mental health follow-up is standard.
key_points: Sertraline is the preferred SSRI in pregnancy with the largest AU evidence base.
Paroxetine has a small first-trimester cardiac malformation signal; avoid if alternatives.
Stopping in recurrent depression: 60-70% relapse within 6 months.
Multidisciplinary perinatal mental health surveillance is standard.
explanation.pearls: Sertraline is the preferred AU pregnancy SSRI; paroxetine is avoided due to first-trimester cardiac malformation signal.

sources.label: RANZCP Perinatal Mental Health Clinical Practice Guideline 2023
COPE Perinatal Mental Health National Guideline

stem:
A 38-year-old with bipolar I disorder has been stable on lithium carbonate 800 mg nocte for the last 4 years. Last week she developed acute gastroenteritis with vomiting and diarrhoea. She has continued her usual lithium dose and is now in your GP clinic with a mild tremor, mild ataxia, and slurred speech.

She has no other regular medications. Her last lithium level 3 months ago was 0.7 mmol/L on the morning trough (target 0.6-0.8). She has had no recent change in fluid or salt intake apart from the gastroenteritis and is mentally lucid.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 90, BP 112/68 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Coarse tremor, mild gait ataxia, dysarthria, no nystagmus, alert and orientated, no clonus
data_table.Investigations: Bedside only, no lithium level drawn yet

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Withhold the next dose, urgent serum lithium level and UEC at the same blood draw
options.rationale: Acute illness with volume loss reduces lithium clearance and precipitates toxicity; the AU step is to withhold the dose and measure both lithium and renal function immediately because clinical features alone underestimate risk. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Continue full-dose lithium and increase oral fluid intake to flush the drug through
options.rationale: Continuing full-dose lithium in dehydration risks progressive toxicity; oral fluids without dose adjustment or measurement are inadequate. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Reduce the lithium dose by half empirically and review the patient in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Empirical dose halving without a serum level and renal function is unsafe; lithium has a narrow therapeutic window and the level may already be in toxic range. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Stop lithium permanently and start sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily today
options.rationale: Permanent cessation after a single episode of suspected toxicity is excessive; lithium is generally restarted at a reduced dose after the acute illness resolves and renal function recovers. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Refer immediately to the renal team for haemodialysis without measuring the level
options.rationale: Haemodialysis is reserved for levels >2.5 mmol/L, severe neurological features, or renal failure; you cannot triage to dialysis without measuring the lithium level. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: Lithium toxicity precipitates in dehydration, renal impairment, NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors or thiazide diuretics, low-salt diet, and intercurrent illness. Mild toxicity (1.5-2.0 mmol/L) presents with tremor, ataxia, dysarthria and gastrointestinal symptoms; moderate (2.0-2.5) adds confusion and myoclonus; severe (>2.5) brings seizures, coma, and arrhythmia. Withhold lithium, measure serum level and UEC, treat the underlying volume loss, and arrange admission if symptomatic or level high. Haemodialysis indicated for level >2.5, neurological compromise, or renal failure.
key_points: Lithium toxicity precipitants: dehydration, NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, thiazides.
Therapeutic 0.6-1.0; toxicity at 1.5+ mmol/L.
Mild: tremor, ataxia, dysarthria. Severe: seizures, coma, renal failure.
Dialysis: level >2.5, severe neurological features, or renal failure.
explanation.pearls: Mild lithium toxicity at 1.5-2.0 mmol/L presents with tremor, ataxia and dysarthria; gastroenteritis is a classic precipitant.

stem:
A 22-year-old presents to the emergency department 90 minutes after ingesting approximately 50 amitriptyline 25 mg tablets in a suicide attempt. She is drowsy but rousable. She has had one self-terminating generalised seizure in the ambulance.

On arrival she is intubated for airway protection. Her ECG shows sinus tachycardia at 130 bpm with a QRS duration of 130 milliseconds and a terminal R wave in aVR of 4 mm. Bedside glucose is normal, paracetamol level is sent, and she has IV access in place.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 130, BP 90/56 mmHg, RR 12 (ventilated), SpO2 98% on 40% FiO2, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Intubated and sedated, GCS prior to intubation 9, dry mucous membranes, sluggish pupils, hyperreflexia
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus tachycardia QRS 130 ms with aVR terminal R 4 mm, VBG pH 7.30 pCO2 38 mmHg HCO3 19 mmol/L (22-32), sodium 138 mmol/L (135-145), paracetamol pending
data_table.Medications: Currently nil; previously amitriptyline 25 mg prescribed for chronic pain

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological intervention?

options.text: Intravenous flumazenil 0.2 mg bolus, repeated every minute to reverse drowsiness
options.rationale: Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines, not tricyclic antidepressants; it is also contraindicated in TCA co-ingestion because it lowers the seizure threshold. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Intravenous physostigmine 1 mg bolus to reverse central and peripheral antimuscarinic effect
options.rationale: Physostigmine is contraindicated in TCA overdose because it can precipitate seizures and asystole; it is reserved for isolated pure antimuscarinic toxidromes. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg bolus, repeated to a target QRS <120 ms
options.rationale: TCA cardiotoxicity from sodium-channel blockade is reversed by sodium bicarbonate boluses; an indication is QRS >100 ms or features such as terminal R wave in aVR >3 mm or refractory hypotension. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Intravenous activated charcoal 50 g via the nasogastric tube once airway is protected
options.rationale: Activated charcoal in TCA overdose is considered only within 1 hour of ingestion and in an airway-protected patient; at 90 minutes the cardiotoxicity is the priority and bicarbonate should not be delayed. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

options.text: Intravenous lidocaine infusion 1 mg/kg load then 1-4 mg/min for tachyarrhythmia control
options.rationale: Class IB antiarrhythmic alone does not address the underlying sodium-channel blockade; lidocaine is a second-line option for ventricular arrhythmias after bicarbonate, not first-line. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Toxicology)

explanation.summary: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose causes sodium-channel blockade (wide QRS, terminal R in aVR, hypotension), antimuscarinic effects (mydriasis, urinary retention, ileus), seizures, and CNS depression. The cornerstone of cardiotoxic management is intravenous sodium bicarbonate, indicated when QRS >100 ms, terminal R wave in aVR >3 mm, refractory hypotension, or ventricular arrhythmia. Target QRS <120 ms and serum pH 7.50-7.55. Class IA, IC, and III antiarrhythmics are contraindicated. Refractory cases may require lipid emulsion or VA-ECMO.
key_points: TCA cardiotoxicity: QRS >100 ms, aVR terminal R >3 mm, refractory hypotension.
Sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg IV bolus, repeat to target QRS <120 ms.
Target serum pH 7.50-7.55.
Avoid class IA, IC, III antiarrhythmics; avoid physostigmine.
explanation.pearls: TCA overdose: sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg when QRS >100 ms or terminal R in aVR >3 mm.

stem:
A 28-year-old with schizophrenia has been on olanzapine 15 mg nocte for 8 months with full symptomatic remission. At his routine review you note he has gained 12 kg, his waist circumference is now 110 cm, fasting glucose is 6.5 mmol/L, and lipids show triglycerides 2.8 mmol/L and HDL 0.8 mmol/L. He is sedentary, eats takeaway most evenings, but does not drink alcohol.

He asks about whether his medication should change. He has had one previous relapse on quetiapine following self-cessation 2 years ago. He has no diabetes, no cardiovascular disease, and no extrapyramidal features.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 86, BP 138/86 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 98%, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: BMI 32 kg/m^2 (baseline 28), waist 110 cm, no EPS, no parkinsonism, no akathisia
data_table.Investigations: Fasting glucose 6.5 mmol/L (3.5-5.4), HbA1c 6.0%, triglycerides 2.8 mmol/L (<2.0), HDL 0.8 mmol/L (>1.0), LDL 3.5 mmol/L, prolactin within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate medication strategy?

options.text: Cross-taper olanzapine to risperidone 4 mg mane to improve the metabolic profile
options.rationale: Risperidone has intermediate metabolic burden and high prolactin elevation; aripiprazole offers a better metabolic profile while preserving efficacy. (Source: RANZCP Antipsychotic Maintenance Guideline 2022)

options.text: Reduce olanzapine dose to 5 mg nocte and continue current lifestyle advice only
options.rationale: Dose reduction may modestly help but a substantial proportion of metabolic burden persists even at lower doses; switching is the AU recommendation when metabolic syndrome is established. (Source: RANZCP Antipsychotic Maintenance Guideline 2022)

options.text: Continue olanzapine at current dose and add metformin 500 mg twice daily for glucose
options.rationale: Metformin addition helps offset weight gain but does not address the dyslipidaemia or the overall metabolic profile; switching to a lower-burden antipsychotic is preferred when relapse risk allows. (Source: RANZCP Antipsychotic Maintenance Guideline 2022)

options.text: Cross-taper olanzapine to aripiprazole 15 mg morning while monitoring relapse risk
options.rationale: Aripiprazole has the lowest metabolic burden among atypicals and is the AU preferred switch for olanzapine-related metabolic syndrome in stable patients with relapse risk balanced by partial dopamine agonism. (Source: RANZCP Antipsychotic Maintenance Guideline 2022)

options.text: Switch to clozapine 12.5 mg daily titrated to maintenance dose with weekly bloods
options.rationale: Clozapine is reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia (failed 2 antipsychotics at adequate dose); this patient is well controlled on olanzapine and clozapine has worse metabolic burden anyway. (Source: RANZCP Antipsychotic Maintenance Guideline 2022)

explanation.summary: Atypical antipsychotic metabolic burden ranges from clozapine and olanzapine (highest) through quetiapine and risperidone (intermediate) to aripiprazole, lurasidone and ziprasidone (lowest). Monitor weight, waist, lipids, glucose and prolactin at baseline, 4 weeks, 12 weeks, and annually. When metabolic syndrome develops on olanzapine in a stable patient, the AU preferred switch is to aripiprazole, with a cross-taper over 2-4 weeks because abrupt change risks relapse. Lifestyle interventions, metformin for glycaemic concern, and statin therapy when indicated complement the switch.
key_points: Metabolic burden: clozapine/olanzapine > quetiapine/risperidone > aripiprazole.
Cross-taper olanzapine to aripiprazole over 2-4 weeks.
Add metformin for glycaemic concerns; statin if indicated by AbsCVD risk.
Baseline, 4-week, 12-week, then annual metabolic monitoring.
explanation.pearls: Aripiprazole has the lowest metabolic burden of the atypicals and is the preferred switch when olanzapine causes metabolic syndrome.

stem:
A 26-year-old presents with a 6-week history of elevated mood, reduced sleep need, grandiose plans, and pressure of speech. During this time he believes he is being followed by ASIO operatives and hears their voices commenting on his actions. His mother says these psychotic features have persisted for 3 weeks now without any of the elevated-mood symptoms.

He has had two prior depressive episodes treated by his GP. He has no significant past medical history, no substance use, and no family history of psychosis. His thyroid function and urine drug screen are normal. There is no head trauma history.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 88, BP 122/76 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99%, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Currently mood euthymic, mild psychomotor slowing, persistent persecutory delusion, auditory hallucinations, insight partial
data_table.Investigations: TFTs within normal limits, urine drug screen negative, FBC and UEC within normal limits, MRI brain awaiting

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder, bipolar type, current episode with manic features
options.rationale: DSM-5 schizoaffective disorder requires a mood episode concurrent with criterion A psychosis AND at least 2 weeks of psychotic symptoms without prominent mood symptoms; the 3 weeks of pure psychosis here is the discriminator from bipolar with psychotic features. (Source: DSM-5-TR American Psychiatric Association 2022)

options.text: Bipolar I disorder, current episode manic, with mood-congruent psychotic features
options.rationale: Bipolar I with psychotic features confines psychosis to the mood episode; the 3 weeks of psychosis without mood symptoms in this patient excludes this diagnosis. (Source: DSM-5-TR American Psychiatric Association 2022)

options.text: Schizophrenia, paranoid subtype with comorbid recurrent depressive episodes
options.rationale: Schizophrenia requires criterion A symptoms for at least 6 months and mood episodes are time-limited relative to total illness duration; the prominence of mood episodes here makes schizoaffective the better fit. (Source: DSM-5-TR American Psychiatric Association 2022)

options.text: Major depressive disorder, current severe with psychotic features and mixed mania
options.rationale: MDD with psychotic features confines psychosis to mood episodes and excludes manic episodes; this patient has clear manic features with elevated mood and reduced sleep need. (Source: DSM-5-TR American Psychiatric Association 2022)

options.text: Brief psychotic disorder with marked stressor lasting less than one month duration
options.rationale: Brief psychotic disorder is by definition less than 1 month and resolves with return to premorbid functioning; this patient has been symptomatic for 6 weeks with prior mood episodes. (Source: DSM-5-TR American Psychiatric Association 2022)

explanation.summary: Schizoaffective disorder is distinguished from bipolar disorder with psychotic features by the requirement of at least 2 weeks of criterion A psychotic symptoms in the ABSENCE of prominent mood symptoms during the lifetime course of illness. Mood episodes must also be present for the majority of the total illness duration. Bipolar with psychotic features confines all psychotic symptoms to the mood episode. Schizoaffective subtype is bipolar type if any manic episode has occurred, depressive type otherwise. Treatment combines a mood stabiliser or antipsychotic with episode-specific care.
key_points: Schizoaffective requires ≥2 weeks of psychosis WITHOUT mood symptoms.
Bipolar with psychotic features: psychosis only during mood episodes.
Schizophrenia: mood episodes brief relative to total illness duration.
Schizoaffective subtype is bipolar type if any manic episode.
explanation.pearls: Schizoaffective vs bipolar with psychotic features: ≥2 weeks of psychosis without mood symptoms is the schizoaffective discriminator.

sources.label: RANZCP Clinical Practice Guidelines for Schizoaffective and Related Disorders
